a: Observability is being able to understand what is happening inside a running system just by looking at the signals it gives off, like logs, numbers, and traces. The goal is to answer "why is this broken?" without guessing or adding new code first.
fs: Observability is inferring a system's internal state from its external outputs (logs, metrics, traces) so you can debug what you did not predict.
fj: Thinking observability is just having dashboards. Dashboards show known problems; observability is about answering new questions you did not plan for.

a: A metric is a number measured over time, like CPU usage, request count, or error rate. Each value has a timestamp, so you can chart it and watch it rise or fall. Metrics are cheap to store and great for spotting that something changed.
fs: A metric is a timestamped numeric measurement, ideal for trends and alerting because it aggregates cheaply over time.
fj: Expecting a metric to tell you why something broke. A metric tells you that error rate went up, not the reason behind any single request.

a: A log is a timestamped text record of a single event that happened, like "user 42 logged in" or "payment failed: card declined". Apps write logs as they run, and you read them to see the detailed story of what occurred.
fs: A log is a timestamped, often structured event record that gives the granular context you need to explain a specific failure.
fj: Treating logs as something you only read by eye. At scale you search and filter structured logs; scrolling raw text does not work across thousands of servers.

a: A trace follows one request as it travels through your system, recording each step and how long it took. If a request hits the web app, then a database, then a payment service, the trace shows all three and which one was slow.
fs: A trace stitches together the spans of a single request across services, exposing latency and where time is actually spent.
fj: Confusing a trace with a log. A log is one event; a trace links many steps of the same request into one end-to-end timeline.

a: An alert is an automatic notification that fires when a signal crosses a line you set, like error rate above 5% for 5 minutes. Instead of staring at dashboards all day, the alert pings you (email, Slack, pager) so you can react fast.
fs: An alert is a rule on a metric or log that notifies a human when a threshold is breached, turning passive data into a timely page.
fj: Alerting on everything. Too many noisy alerts get ignored (alert fatigue); good alerts fire on real, actionable problems only.
